summary: Ancient Greek tragedian (~480-406 BCE). Most commonly identified by Medea, The Bacchae, The Trojan Women, Hippolytus, and his appearances as a character in Aristophanes' comedies (The Frogs, Thesmophoriazusae, The Acharnians). In The Frogs, Dionysus judges Euripides' plays inferior to Aeschylus's. Supposedly died in Macedonia after self-imposed exile; legend says torn apart by Molossian hounds. Also wrote The Cyclops, the only surviving complete satyr play.

works.name: As Character in Aristophanes
works.description: Euripides appears frequently as a character in Aristophanes' comedies, particularly The Frogs, Thesmophoriazusae, and The Acharnians.

clues.clue: In The Frogs, Dionysus judges Euripides inferior to Aeschylus in a poetry contest — Euripides's words are lighter on a literal scale
examples: Dionysus judges this man's plays to be inferior to those of Aeschylus
his descriptions of the Argo, persuasion, and an iron club do not weigh as much as the words of his opponent
Sophocles is given this man's chair in Hades after he defeats Euripides in a literary contest

clues.clue: In The Frogs, Aeschylus interrupts Euripides' prologues by shouting 'Lost his little flask of oil!'
examples: keeps saying that this man 'lost his little can of oil' as he tries to recite the prologues
is repeatedly interrupted by a rival who shouts, 'Lost his little flask of oil!'
The poetic device lekythion was coined by this character as he uses the phrase 'lost his little oil flask' to interrupt another character

clues.clue: In The Acharnians, Dicaeopolis borrows a beggar's costume from Euripides (based on Telephus); given rags, cap, staff, chipped cup
examples: goes to this man's house and exasperates him by requesting a beggar's outfit and some vegetables
agrees to give away a beggar's rags, cap, and staff, a chipped cup, and a pot with a sponge

clues.clue: In Thesmophoriazusae, Euripides convinces his relative Mnesilochus to spy on the women-only Thesmophoria festival; Euripides' portrayal of women is discussed
examples: invents this man's fictional relative Mnesilochus, with whom he crashes the women-only Thesmophoria festival
This character convinces Mnesilochus to spy on a women's-only festival
Euripides's portrayal of women in his plays is discussed by women such as Micca in the assembly

clues.clue: Slave says 'he is and he isn't' when asked if Euripides is home (Acharnians); addresses prayer to 'ether, tongue, nose, and wit' (Frogs)
examples: When a man asks whether this character is home, a slave answers that 'he is and he isn't'
addresses a prayer to the ether, his tongue, his nose, and his wit

clues.clue: Aeschylus accuses Euripides of arguing 'life is not life' with depictions of women giving birth in temples and having sex with brothers
examples: accuses another character of arguing that 'life is not life' with his depiction of women giving birth in temples and having sex with their brothers

works.name: Medea
works.description: Tragedy in which Medea kills her children and Glauce after being abandoned by Jason. She escapes on a divine chariot pulled by dragons. Chorus of Corinthian women.

clues.clue: Medea kills her children after being abandoned by Jason; escapes on a golden chariot pulled by dragons (chariot of Helios)
examples: ends as the title character flees on a divine chariot pulled by dragons
the title character kills her and Jason's children
This character uses a chariot of Helios to escape Corinth after killing her children in revenge
ends with the title character leaving the stage in a dragon-pulled chariot

clues.clue: Glauce killed via a poisoned robe/coronet/diadem; messenger recounts her death; chorus of Corinthian women
examples: A chorus of Corinthian women features in this play, in which a messenger recounts Glauce's death via a poisoned robe
uses a poisoned robe and diadem to kill Glauce
sends out two poisoned golden robes as gifts

clues.clue: Medea says she'd 'rather stand three times to face their battles, shield in hand, than bear one child' / 'rather go to war three times than give birth once'
examples: says that she would rather stand 'three times to face their battles, shield in hand, than bear one child!'
rather stand 'three times in battle holding up my shield than give birth once'
'I'd rather go to war three times than give birth once'

clues.clue: Only Ancient Greek play where a character guilty of killing a family member goes unpunished
examples: this only Ancient Greek play in which a character guilty of killing a family member goes unpunished

clues.clue: Medea promises to cure King Aegeus's infertility in exchange for asylum in Athens; oracle tells Aegeus 'not to unstop the wineskin's neck'
examples: promises to cure King Aegeus's infertility in exchange for asylum
a king is given magical fertility drugs and does not understand a riddle about 'unstopping the wineskin's neck'

clues.clue: Adapted by Luis Alfaro (Mojada — Medea as seamstress crossing US-Mexico border), Cherrie Moraga (The Hungry Woman — kills Chac-Mool), Robinson Jeffers (1947, Judith Anderson won Tony), Jean Anouilh (lives in trailer, dies in fire)

works.name: The Bacchae
works.description: Tragedy about Pentheus and the worship of Dionysus. Agave kills her own son Pentheus in a Bacchic frenzy. Euripides' final play.

clues.clue: Dionysus escapes imprisonment — chained to a bull, causes earthquake and fire at the palace

clues.clue: E.R. Dodds authored the still-standard commentary on this play

clues.clue: Pentheus orders Dionysus's hair cut; Dionysus name means 'man of sorrows'; devises false etymology of meros from homeros

works.name: The Trojan Women
works.description: Tragedy about the fate of Hecuba, Andromache, Cassandra, and Helen after the fall of Troy. Part of a trilogy with Alexandros and Palamedes.

works.name: Hippolytus
works.description: Tragedy in which Aphrodite punishes Hippolytus for his chaste devotion to Artemis by making his stepmother Phaedra fall in love with him. Phaedra writes a false suicide note accusing him of rape. Basis for Racine's Phedre.

clues.clue: Aphrodite/Cypris punishes Hippolytus for devotion to Artemis; opens with Aphrodite's monologue explaining her plan
examples: This character angers Aphrodite with his chaste devotion to Artemis

clues.clue: Famous line 'my tongue swore but my heart remained unpledged' — parodied by Aristophanes in The Clouds
examples: Aristophanes riffed on this character's claim that 'my tongue swore but my heart remained unpledged'

examples: laments that men can't buy babies at temples in a misogynistic rant

clues.clue: Basis for Racine's Phedre

works.name: Other Plays

comprehensive_summary:
Euripides (~480-406 BCE) was an ancient Greek tragedian, one of the three great Athenian tragic dramatists alongside Aeschylus and Sophocles. He is most frequently clued through his appearances as a character in Aristophanes' comedies and through his plays Medea, The Bacchae, The Trojan Women, and Hippolytus.

In Aristophanes' The Frogs, Dionysus judges Euripides inferior to Aeschylus in a poetry contest, where a literal scale shows Euripides' descriptions of the Argo and 'iron club' weigh less than Aeschylus's words. Aeschylus interrupts Euripides' prologues by shouting 'Lost his little flask of oil!' (a device called lekythion). Sophocles is given Euripides' chair in Hades. Aeschylus also accuses Euripides of arguing 'life is not life' with his depictions of women giving birth in temples. In The Acharnians, Dicaeopolis borrows a beggar's costume (from the lost play Telephus) from Euripides; a slave says 'he is and he isn't' when asked if Euripides is home. In Thesmophoriazusae, Euripides convinces his relative Mnesilochus to spy on the women-only Thesmophoria festival, where his portrayal of women is debated.

Medea is his most famous play: Medea kills her children after Jason abandons her, has Glauce killed via a poisoned robe and diadem, and escapes on the chariot of Helios pulled by dragons. Unusually, a mortal character appears on the mechane, a position normally reserved for gods. She says she'd 'rather stand three times in battle than give birth once.' She promises to cure King Aegeus's infertility in exchange for asylum in Athens; an oracle tells Aegeus 'not to unstop the wineskin's neck.' The chorus of Corinthian women imagines 'the waters in the sacred rivers are flowing in reverse' and claims 'the mind of man is nothing but a fraud.' Medea predicts Jason will suffer a 'coward's end' from wreckage of his ship. It is the only Greek play where a family murderer goes unpunished. The play opens with a nurse lamenting outside the house. Modern adaptations include Luis Alfaro's Mojada (Medea as seamstress crossing the US-Mexico border), Cherrie Moraga's The Hungry Woman (she kills Chac-Mool), Robinson Jeffers's 1947 version (Judith Anderson won a Tony), and Jean Anouilh's version (Medea lives in a trailer and dies in a fire). Countee Cullen published his translation alongside 'Scottsboro, Too, Is Worth Its Song.'

In The Bacchae, Euripides' final play, Pentheus is convinced to crossdress to spy on women who draw milk and honey from the ground with thyrsi. Dionysus bends a fir tree so Pentheus can sit in it. When captured, Dionysus is chained to a bull but causes an earthquake and fire to escape. Pentheus sees 'two suns' in the sky and horns on Dionysus's head. His mother Agave kills him in a Bacchic frenzy, believing she's ripping apart a mountain lion; she brings the 'lion's head' to her father Cadmus. A messenger describes the maenads performing sparagmos on Cithaeron. The chorus sings 'What is wisdom?' Pentheus's name means 'man of sorrows.' E.R. Dodds authored the still-standard commentary. Tiresias and Cadmus beg Pentheus to sympathize with the Bacchants. Wole Soyinka adapted it as a 'Communion Rite' with a second chorus of slaves set amid Nigerian civil unrest, where blood from Pentheus's head transforms into wine. Joe Orton's Erpingham Camp and Richard Schechner's Dionysus in 69 are also adaptations. It won the prize at the Great Dionysia posthumously.

The Trojan Women describes the fate of Hecuba, Andromache, Cassandra, and Helen after Troy's fall. The herald Talthybius brings in the corpse of baby Astyanax, thrown from the city walls and buried in Hector's shield. It opens with Poseidon and Athena discussing punishment for Ajax the Lesser's rape. Cassandra enters singing a frenzied wedding hymn brandishing flaming torches. It is considered a commentary on the Athenian massacre at Milos. It formed a trilogy with Alexandros and Palamedes; Xenocles bafflingly won first prize over it at the 415 BC City Dionysia. Hecuba is told 'Lift thy head, unhappy lady, from the ground' and unusually prays to a god as 'the fixed law of Nature or man's Mind.' Talthybius vaguely relays that Polyxena will be sacrificed at Achilles' tomb. Jean-Paul Sartre's final stage work was an adaptation. Femi Osofisan adapted it as Women of Owu; Anne Carson created a comic book version with Rosanna Bruno. Plutarch says the tyrant Alexander of Pherae left a performance in shame.

Hippolytus centers on Aphrodite's punishment of the chaste, misogynistic Hippolytus for devotion to Artemis. The play opens with Aphrodite (Cypris) explaining her plan. A nurse pleads on behalf of Phaedra, who is starving herself. Phaedra writes a false suicide note accusing Hippolytus of rape. Theseus uses one of three curses from Poseidon; a monstrous bull from the sea capsizes Hippolytus's chariot and kills him. His famous line 'my tongue swore but my heart remained unpledged' was parodied by Aristophanes. He delivers a misogynistic rant lamenting men can't buy babies at temples. In Roman tradition, he is resurrected by Asclepius and reborn as Virbius. He had a cult where girls about to marry offered locks of hair. Racine's Phedre is based on this play.

Other plays include Orestes (contains the only surviving fragment of ancient Greek music, reconstructed by Armand D'Angour; Apollo arrives as deus ex machina), Alcestis (Heracles ambushes Thanatos and resurrects the title character; adapted as Edufa by Efua Sutherland), Ion (tragicomedy at Delphi), The Phoenician Women (Menoeceus commits suicide; condemns Eteocles), Heracles (adapted by Anne Carson as H of H Playbook), Helen (title character escapes Egyptian king Theoclymenus), Andromache (agon with Hermione), Hecuba (blinds Polymestor), The Children of Herakles, The Cyclops (only surviving complete satyr play), and Iphigenia in Aulis. He supposedly went to live in a cave on Salamis after two bad marriages, died at Archelaus's court in Macedonia, and was torn apart by Molossian hounds according to legend. Plutarch says his verses persuaded Spartans not to destroy Athens. Nietzsche discussed him in The Birth of Tragedy. A HUAC member once asked Hallie Flanagan about what 'Mr Euripides' preached.
recursive_suggestions: Medea — has its own answerline with many tossups
The Bacchae — has its own answerline
The Trojan Women — has its own answerline
The Frogs (Aristophanes) — major source of Euripides-as-character clues

Play: Cassandra enters singing frenzied wedding hymn brandishing flaming torches
Play: part of trilogy with Alexandros and Palamedes; Xenocles won first prize over it at 415 BC Dionysia
Play: Sartre's final stage work was an adaptation; Femi Osofisan adapted as Women of Owu
Play: Aphrodite punishes title character for chaste devotion to Artemis; opens with her monologue
Play: stepmother Phaedra falsely accuses title character of rape in suicide note
Play: Poseidon sends monstrous bull from sea to crash title character's chariot; one of three curses from Poseidon to Theseus
Play: contains line 'my tongue swore but my heart remained unpledged,' parodied by Aristophanes
Playwright: in The Frogs, Dionysus judges him inferior to Aeschylus on a literal scale
Playwright: Aeschylus interrupts his prologues with 'Lost his little flask of oil!' (lekythion)
Playwright: in The Acharnians, Dicaeopolis borrows a beggar's costume from him
Playwright: slave says 'he is and he isn't' when asked if he's home
Playwright: in Thesmophoriazusae, convinces relative Mnesilochus to spy on the women-only Thesmophoria festival
Play: title character kills Lycus then murders his own wife and children; Anne Carson adapted as H of H Playbook
Play: Orestes contains only surviving fragment of ancient Greek music; Apollo arrives as deus ex machina
Play: only surviving complete satyr play; set near Mt. Etna with Odysseus and Silenus
Play: Heracles ambushes Thanatos and resurrects title character; adapted by Efua Sutherland as Edufa
Play: Phoenician Women — Menoeceus commits suicide; condemns Eteocles for retaining throne; Jocasta suicides after sons' duel
Playwright: died at Archelaus's court in Macedonia; torn apart by Molossian hounds
Playwright: Plutarch says his verses persuaded Spartans not to destroy Athens
Playwright: fled to a cave on the island of Salamis; supposedly went there after two bad marriages
